the_twin_engines: Earnings Growth: The primary driver. A business must demonstrate organic, sustainable revenue and earnings expansion over decades, not just quarters.

Multiple Expansion: The secondary driver. Buying a great business at a fair or low P/E multiple allows the stock to compound faster as the market eventually recognizes its quality and bids the multiple up.
quantitative_metrics: The Acorn Principle: Target companies with a Market Cap under $2 Billion. The company must be small enough to have a massive runway for 100x expansion.
The Compounding Engine: Return on Invested Capital (ROIC) consistently > 15%. This proves the business can reinvest its own cash profitably.
Growth Momentum: Organic Revenue Growth consistently > 10% annually.
The Moat Indicator: Gross Margins > 30%, proving the company possesses pricing power and does not compete in a race-to-the-bottom commodity market.
The Dividend Leak: Target companies with No or Low Dividend Yields (< 1%). Every dollar paid out in dividends is a dollar not reinvested at the company's high ROIC.
qualitative_signals: Skin in the Game: Insider/Founder ownership must be > 10%. You want owner-operators whose personal net worth is tied to the long-term equity, not managers chasing quarterly bonuses.
Capital Allocation: Look for 'Outsider CEOs' who excel at strategic, opportunistic share repurchases when the stock is undervalued, thereby accelerating per-share value.
The Coffee-Can Philosophy: The holding period to achieve a 100-bagger averages 17 to 25 years. The biggest gains are made in the waiting, not the trading.
